title: The Ruy Lopez
summary: The oldest serious opening in chess

steps.title: The Spanish
steps.text:
Named after the 16th-century Spanish priest **Ruy López de Segura**, the Ruy Lopez (also called the *Spanish Game*) is the oldest serious opening still played at the highest levels of chess.

Every world champion has played it — Steinitz, Lasker, Capablanca, Alekhine, Botvinnik, Fischer, Karpov, Kasparov, Carlsen.

## The defining sequence

The canonical line is {{e4}} {{e5}} {{Nf3}} {{Nc6}} {{Bb5}}. The bishop pins the c6 knight to the e5 pawn — but the *real* threat is long-term, not immediate.

## What each side wants

- **White's plan:** maintain pressure on e5, develop with **{{c3}}**, **{{O-O}}**, **{{Re1}}**, **{{d4}}**, then expand on the kingside or center.
- **Black's plan:** challenge the bishop with **...a6** (the Morphy Defense), develop with **...Nf6**, **...Be7**, **0-0**, **...d6**, and either hold the e5 pawn (Closed Ruy) or release it for piece activity (Open Ruy).
- **Pawn structure:** characteristic Ruy Lopez chains — **e4-d3** for White vs **e5-d6** for Black, with manoeuvring on both wings.

## Why it's still relevant

The Ruy Lopez creates **strategic depth without forcing tactics**. It's a complete chess education in one opening — pawn structures, piece manoeuvres, attack and defense.

> Once you understand the Ruy Lopez, you understand classical chess.

steps.prompt: Open with the king's pawn — play **{{e4}}**.
steps.hint: 1.e4 stakes the center and is the gateway to the Ruy Lopez.

steps.prompt: Develop with **{{Nf3}}** — attacking e5 and preparing castling.
steps.hint: Nf3 is the knight move every classical opening starts with — it develops a piece AND attacks Black's central pawn.

steps.prompt: The defining move: play **{{Bb5}}** — the bishop steps out to pressure the c6 knight.
steps.hint: The Bb5 pin is *indirect*: it doesn't immediately win the e5 pawn (because of ...Bxc6 dxc6 Nxe5 Qd4). It just slowly squeezes.

steps.title: Main Ideas
steps.text:
After **3.Bb5**, you'll often see beginners think *I can just take the knight!* — and discover that {{Bxc6}} **dxc6** {{Nxe5}} loses to **...Qd4!**, forking the knight and the e4 pawn. The Ruy Lopez is **not** about winning a pawn directly.

## What the Bb5 pin actually achieves

- It threatens to **damage Black's pawn structure** if and when {{Bxc6}} is played.
- It indirectly **undermines e5** — if Black ever lets White play {{Bxc6}} and then {{Nxe5}} without the **...Qd4** trick available, White wins a pawn.
- It limits Black's development — the c6 knight has to think before moving.

## Black's main responses

- **3...a6** (Morphy Defense, by far the most common) — kicks the bishop, asks it to commit. White retreats with **4.Ba4** or trades with **4.Bxc6**.
- **3...Nf6** (Berlin Defense) — Vladimir Kramnik used this to defeat Garry Kasparov for the world title in 2000.
- **3...d6** (Steinitz Defense) — solid but passive.
- **3...Bc5** (Classical) — bold, ignores the pin, prepares ...Nf6 and 0-0.

## A model main line

{{e4}} {{e5}} {{Nf3}} {{Nc6}} {{Bb5}} **a6** **{{Ba4}}** **Nf6** **{{O-O}}** **Be7** **{{Re1}}** **b5** **{{Bb3}}** **d6** **{{c3}}** **0-0** — the classical Closed Ruy.

> The Ruy Lopez is slow chess at its finest. Pieces shuffle, plans crystallize, and one tiny inaccuracy can decide the game ten moves later.
